query: optimal anti-rotational design in surgical blades

retrieved_docs.title: Reverse Shoulder Arthroplasty -- Technique or Treatment -- Humeral Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The humeral head is resected slightly below the greater tuberosity tip. Intramedullary or extramedullary guides can guide the humeral head resection depending on the surgeon's preference. Resection guides are specific for each prosthesis type and have different resection angles. The humeral head is usually resected at about 30 degrees of retroversion. The forearm axis with the elbow flexed at 90 degrees is used as a reference axis for retroversion. [29] The humeral canal is reamed using different reamer sizes and then prepared by humeral stem broaches. A version rod could be attached to the broach handle to monitor the version during humerus preparation. Two types of humeral stems are available, whether press-fit cementless or cemented. The size of the press-fit cementless stem is equal to the size of the final humeral broach size. The cemented stem size is smaller by 2 mm compared to the final broach size, allowing space for the cement around the stem.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasoseptal Flap -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: While a variety of endoscopes should be available, the flap is typically elevated with visualization provided by a 0 or 30-degree endoscope. An endoscopic monopolar electrocautery instrument with a needle-tip at a 45-degree bend provides ergonomic and precise incisions with simultaneous hemostasis. A Cottle elevator, micro-scissor, ball-tip probe, curette, curved olive-tip suction, through-cutting forceps (both straight and angled), and Blakesley forceps (both straight and angled) should be readily available and are typically found on a standard Functional Endoscopic Sinus Surgery (FESS) tray. These instruments allow for atraumatic handling and refining of the flap as it is raised.

retrieved_docs.title: Minimally Invasive Aortic Valve Surgery -- Technique or Treatment -- Ministernotomy Approach
retrieved_docs.content: The patient is positioned supine on the operating table with the customary sterile draping. A 5 to 6 cm incision is made over the upper midline, exposing the manubrium down to the right second through the fourth intercostal space. This area is dissected away from the sternum, and a "j" shaped pattern for the saw is marked using bovie cautery. While a reciprocating bone saw is an option, many prefer an oscillating one. A retractor is placed, ensuring gentle retraction to prevent injury to the right internal mammary artery; vessel ligation can be performed in case of injury or concerns. The thymic fat pad is divided and resected to enhance exposure. An inverted T-shaped incision in the pericardium provides optimal exposure, with pericardial sutures placed for excellent visibility. Standard aortic cannulation, 2-stage venous cannulation in the right atrium, right superior pulmonary vein vent placement, and an antegrade cardioplegia needle in the ascending aorta are performed. If exposure is insufficient or the j-shaped manubrial technique is used, femoral-femoral cannulation is an option. The standard-size cross-clamp is suitable, although a mini low-profile clamp may offer more space. Both bioprosthetic and mechanical valves can be placed with this technique.

retrieved_docs.title: Rhinoplasty -- Technique or Treatment -- Incisions and Exposure
retrieved_docs.content: Open approach: The transcolumellar incision is marked using a stair-step or chevron shape ("inverted-V") to break up the contour of the scar and direct the contractile forces obliquely across the columella to minimize the chance of notching (see Image . Rhinoplasty Incisions). If a chevron-shaped incision is used, the chevron should be at least 3 mm in width at its base to prevent it from flattening during healing and the base should be level with the narrowest portion of the columella, which is where the skin is thinnest and least likely to produce a prominent scar. The incision itself is made with a fine blade, such as a Bard-Parker #11 or #15c, or a #6700 beaver blade, taking care to avoid damaging the underlying medial crura of the lower lateral cartilages. A columellar flap is then elevated up to the domes of the lower lateral cartilages using fine scissors (Wilmer, Converse, iris, etc.). The columellar vessels and intercrural soft tissues are isolated and divided with bipolar electrocautery, thereby exposing the medial surfaces of the medial crura. With a combination of 1 wide and 2 narrow double-pronged skin hooks, the dome of the lower lateral cartilage is flattened and held under tension while the marginal incision is made, continuing laterally from the transcolumellar incision (see Image . Open Rhinoplasty Approach). Fine scissors or a scalpel can be used for this incision. The contralateral marginal incision is then made identically. Two wide double-pronged skin hooks are then placed, one pulling upward under the SSTE and one retracting downward under the domes, so that the soft tissue can be cleared from the superficial surface of the lower and upper lateral cartilages, taking care to leave the perichondrium intact. Either scissors or cotton-tipped applicators can be used for this elevation. With the lower two-thirds of the nasal skeleton exposed, an elevator (Joseph, Cottle, or similar) is used to dissect the periosteum from the nasal bones and the frontal processes of the maxillae, thus completing the open approach.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Management of Femoral Neck Fractures -- Preparation -- Positioning During Sliding Hip Screw and Cannulated Screw Fixation
retrieved_docs.content: For these procedures, the patient is placed supine on a radiolucent traction table. The foot on the side to be operated on must be in a traction boot. The unaffected side is placed in the lithotomy position, strapped in a leg holder, and placed in flexion, abduction, and external rotation to ensure the radiographic C-arm can access the fracture site appropriately. Gentle traction and internal rotation should be applied to reduce the fracture. Slight abduction may be needed to reduce any varus malalignment. The fracture should be nearly anatomically aligned, with radiographic confirmation, before prepping and draping. Open reduction may be necessary if closed reduction is not possible. Correct lower extremity rotational alignment is imperative. The patient should be positioned with the patella facing toward the ceiling. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Rhinoplasty -- Technique or Treatment -- Septoplasty
retrieved_docs.content: If the septoplasty is performed via an open rhinoplasty approach, disarticulating the upper lateral cartilages from the dorsal septum early in the process will substantially improve visualization and make room for later spreader graft placement. Once the septal flaps are elevated bilaterally, the deviated bone and cartilage are resected, or straight cartilage is harvested as necessary. The cartilage is often incised with a Freer septal knife, a #15 blade, or a Ballenger swivel knife, while bony cuts are typically made with Gorney double-action nasal shears, Jansen-Middleton rongeurs, and osteotomes (see Image . Septal Cartilage Harvest). It is critical to leave at least 10 to 15 mm of cartilage intact along the dorsal and caudal aspects of the septum (the "L-strut") to provide enough support to the nose to prevent a postoperative saddle deformity. Critical to this is maintaining the integrity of the bony-cartilaginous junction at the keystone, although the bony-cartilaginous junction at the anterior nasal spine is also important for tip support. If the L-strut itself is deviated, grafting techniques may be required to straighten it, and these are often more easily employed via an open rhinoplasty approach. For a more in-depth discussion of septoplasty, please see the following article: Septoplasty .

retrieved_docs.title: Aortic Valve Replacement -- Introduction -- History of Valvular Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Medtronic Hall valve developed by Dr. Karl Hall and Robert Kaster is the most common tilting disc valve. Perforation in the middle of the disc served as a guide and an improved tilting angle. [23] In 1977, St Jude Medical developed Bi-leaflet valve, which comprised two semi-circular flaps with hinges close to the center of the orifice. Stagnation of blood flow leading to thrombus formation around the hinge region was a problem with the bi-leaflet valve. [15] A newly developed valve resolved this issue with a valve in which the continuous 360-degree rotation around the central axis of the valve ensured an even distribution of blood flow and prevent thrombus formation. [16] Predisposition for thrombosis, embolism, need for lifelong anticoagulation therapy are drawbacks of mechanical valves.

retrieved_docs.title: Reverse Shoulder Arthroplasty -- Technique or Treatment -- Glenoid Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Identifying the lower glenoid rim for proper base plate seating is important. Inferiorly placed base plate with 10 degrees inferior tilt was shown to reduce the base plate failure. [33] A central pilot wire is inserted to guide the glenoid reamer. The glenoid reamer is applied along the guidewire to prepare the glenoid surface and expose the subchondral bone for adequate seating of the base plate. Glenoid reaming could be performed at 0 or 10 degrees of inferior tilt. An inferiorly tilted position for the glenoid component increases the deltoid muscle tension due to inferior displacement of the humerus and reduces early glenoid failure. Still, it does not reduce the incidence of scapular notching. [33] [34]  The pilot wire is then removed from the glenoid. The base plate holder is used to apply for the glenoid plate, and it is flushed to the bone. The base is compressed to the exposed glenoid subchondral bone by the central screw. Superior and inferior screws aiming for dense bone are inserted to secure the base plate and reduce the micromotion at the plate-bone interface.

retrieved_docs.title: Mohs Micrographic Surgery Interpretation of Technical Problems Impacting Slide Quality -- Issues of Concern -- Cutting
retrieved_docs.content: Epithelial cells are 8 µm thick on average, so optimal cutting typically ranges from 4 to 6 µm. All tissue, especially cartilage, may adhere better to dry, positively charged slides. An adhesive can also help keep cartilage on the slide. Cutting in a constant motion and using either a delicate brush or the antiroll plate may help facilitate the cutting of a thin, flat section that can be placed on a slide. [5] Despite adequate 12-µm thick sections, these cuts are darker than the typical thinner sections (see Images. Thick Sections A and B). Though not represented well in the images, fine nuclear and cytoplasmic details on very high power are more obscured with thicker sections. Specimens measuring 3 µm are ultra-thin, very faint, and difficult to interpret (see Images. Thin Section A and B). In addition, cutting sections too thin can be challenging for a histology technician to prepare and may result in the specimen "chunking" out of the block. [12] Cutting with a damaged microtome blade may result in significant tears in the tissue (see Images. Damaged Microtome Blade A and B).

retrieved_docs.content: Scalpel (#15 blade) Forceps Dissecting scissors such as tenotomy scissors Suture scissors Skin hooks Bipolar electrocautery and topical thrombin Normal saline solution for washout Sutures: size and type depend upon the anatomical location and surgeon preference (eg, absorbable for deep dermal and nonabsorbable for simple interrupted)

retrieved_docs.title: Diskectomy -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical aspects include adherence to safety protocols, maintaining a sterile environment in the operating theater, administering antibiotic prophylaxis, and ensuring proper positioning. This involves placing the patient in the genupectoral position to free the abdomen and provide chest support for stability. Eye protection should also be implemented. The shoulders should be abducted to 90°, while the elbows should be flexed to 90°, creating a kyphotic curve in the spine. This facilitates the opening of the inter-laminar space and provides optimal access to the surgical site. Lastly, localization and exposure techniques, guided by surface landmarks and fluoroscopy, ensure accurate targeting and access to the affected disk space, facilitating surgical intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Orbital Atherectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: With all equipment assembled and personal in place, the atherectomy procedure can be initiated. Start with obtaining arterial vascular access using a 6 Fr sheath or larger using the provider's preferred approach. Access the lesion with a guide catheter, and use angiography, IVUS, or OCT to localize and evaluate the target lesion. Once located, cross the lesion with the coronary guidewire. Prepare the lubricant, connect the OAD to the OAS, and prime the pump. Before inserting the OAD into the vessel, perform testing to ensure appropriate crown advancement via advancer knob and crown rotation using the on/off button. Verify the flow of saline with crown rotation. Ensure the brake lever is open, and advance the OAD drive shaft over the guidewire. Using fluoroscopy, advance the crown to approximately 10 mm proximal to the target lesion. Verify the OAD distal tip is beyond the lesion. Press down on the brake lever to engage the brake and press the on/off button to activate crown rotation at low speed (80,000 rpm), which can be verified via the LED light on the OAD.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Management of Femoral Neck Fractures -- Technique or Treatment -- Sliding Hip Screw
retrieved_docs.content: The guidewire is then over-drilled with a triple reamer to create a channel for both the lag screw and plate barrel. Reaming extends to the depth selected for the screw. Radiography must show that the reamer's 3rd part has engaged with the bone cortex, and the guidewire is not advancing into the pelvis.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cerclage -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The most common technique for performing transvaginal cervical cerclage is the McDonald method. First applied in 1951, this procedure involves a simple purse-string suture at the cervicovaginal junction. Under regional anesthesia, the patient is placed in the dorsal lithotomy position and prepped with a vaginal betadine solution. A speculum or right-angle retractors are used to adequately visualize the cervix. The anterior lip of the cervix may be gently grasped using ring polyp forceps, and the vesicocervical junction should be identified. Just anterior to this junction, a nonabsorbable suture is inserted into the cervix in a purse-string manner, taking caution to avoid the paracervical vessels. The suture is then tied down with a surgeon knot, either anterior or posterior. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotation Flaps -- Technique or Treatment -- Unique Rotation Flaps
retrieved_docs.content: The spiral flap has several proposed uses, including lower eyelid and ventral fingertip defects. [14] [15] However, the original indication for a spiral flap was for deep defects along the alar groove measuring up to 1.5 cm in size. This flap is useful for the ala because it has the potential to preserve the alar groove or sulcus. The flap is incised similarly to a classic rotation flap. However, because of the spiral shape, there are additional variables to consider, including the curvature of the spiral. Defects involving the alar groove should use a spiral where the width of each level of the spiral is the same. This will produce a prominent inward bend that will recreate the groove. Defects along the alar crease will require a shallower depression and should use an algorithmic spiral where each level of the spiral becomes progressively larger. The first arm of the spiral is responsible for the recreation of the alar crease or groove. To properly align this, the first arm of the spiral must have a width equal to the distance from the inferior aspect of the defect to the desired location of the alar groove. This arm will be sutured to the rest of the flap, thus initiating the spiral. The tissue for this flap can be taken from the nasal sidewall or an incision directed along the alar groove. [14] [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.content: inversely related to artificial rupture of membranes to assist delivery, reinforcing the role of membrane rupture in surgical induction strategies. Induction by cervical dilation is related to تحفيز المخاض (induction of labor) and may be synonymous with other procedures to assist delivery, highlighting its overlap with other assisted‑delivery techniques. Procedural techniques specific to fetal positioning and extraction are also integrated. Manual rotation of the fetal head is classified as an inverse‑ISA of Procedures on Head, employs the method termed التداول (turning), and targets the fetal head as the direct site for both manual rotation and Delivery by double application of forceps. Extraction is identified as a method of Vaginal cesarean section, linking a specific extraction technique to that mode of cesarean delivery. Obstetrical trachelotomy is listed as an other delivery procedure, synonymous with vaginal cesarean section, and is a subtype of dilation/incision of cervix –

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 112
retrieved_docs.content: with “Procedure on organ” also serving as an inverse‑ISA of fetal monitoring, thereby integrating organ‑based procedures within the fetal monitoring framework. This comprehensive, multilingual network clarifies how preventive, diagnostic, therapeutic and operative techniques—ranging from fetal monitoring and chorionic villus sampling to complex version maneuvers, forceps delivery, cervical cerclage, ectopic pregnancy surgery and hysterectomy—are classified, interrelated, and applied in obstetric patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 156
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical Manipulation is the primary obstetric category that integrates therapeutic applications, all forms of obstetrical version (combined, external, internal and manual rotation), a range of assisted‑delivery techniques, cervical and membrane interventions, induction strategies, and pregnancy‑termination procedures, thereby supporting comprehensive ante‑partum, intrapartum and post‑partum care. Combined obstetrical version is a subclass of Surgical Manipulation and serves as the parent of internal and combined version without extraction; internal obstetrical version and external obstetrical version are direct children of Surgical Manipulation. Manual rotation of the fetal head, delivery by double application of forceps, delivery of a vertex presentation, extraction, and vacuum‑assisted delivery (الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين) are grouped under “other delivery procedures,” classified as بضع الارتفاق (obstetric maneuvers) and as other procedures to assist delivery. The obstetric

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2909
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery by double application of forceps is a obstetric procedure in which the fetal head serves as the direct site of the intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2266
retrieved_docs.content: Cesarean hysterectomy, Intrapartum Fetal Stimulation, and Manual Fetal Rotation are all classified as إجراءات توليدية (obstetric procedures). Cesarean hysterectomy is a surgical obstetric intervention performed during a cesarean delivery when removal of the uterus is required, often to control severe postpartum hemorrhage or manage abnormal placental attachment. Intrapartum Fetal Stimulation is a bedside obstetric technique used during labor to assess fetal well‑being or to provoke fetal movements, helping clinicians evaluate fetal responsiveness and guide management decisions. Manual Fetal Rotation is a hands‑on obstetric maneuver employed to rotate a malpositioned fetal head into a more favorable orientation for vaginal delivery, thereby reducing the risk of obstructed labor and associated complications. Together, these إجراءات توليدية illustrate the range of operative and non‑operative strategies obstetricians use to address complex maternal‑fetal situations, optimize delivery outcomes, and enhance patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 78
retrieved_docs.content: the fetus; the first three are subclasses of Internal and combined version without extraction, an “Other delivery procedure” performed in the uterus, while Delivery by double application of forceps targets the fetal head. Manual conversion of position also has the uterus as its procedural site. The Arabic term التداول (fetal turning) refers to Manual rotation of the fetal head, a Procedure on Head (a form of جراحة) performed on the fetal head and grouped with other delivery procedures. Manual rotation of the fetal head, External obstetrical version, obstetrical trachelotomy (synonymous with vaginal cesarean section) and Induction of labor by artificial rupture of membranes are children of the category “Other delivery procedures,” itself classified as بضع الارتفاق (Other procedures to assist delivery). Obstetrical trachelotomy is an incision of the uterine cervix, related to preventive cervical incision, and is also an Other delivery procedure. Dilation of the cervix uteri and vacuum

Surgical Manipulation is a higher‑level category that includes operative obstetric techniques: delivery by double application of forceps, delivery of vertex presentation, external obstetrical version, internal obstetrical version, combined obstetrical version (with or without extraction), manual conversion of position, face delivery, and the Arabic procedure الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين (delivery by fetal suction). These interventions act directly on the fetal head and are linked to manual fetal‑positioning maneuvers (Arabic التداول “turning”) and to artificial rupture of membranes (AROM) and membrane sweeping, which are primary strategies for

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: version without extraction, external obstetrical version, double‑forceps delivery, manual rotation of the fetal head, failed forceps delivery (فشل التوليد بالملقط), cervical dilation, and vacuum aspiration of products of conception—the broader class for evacuation of retained products of conception (تفريغ منتجات الحمل المحتجزة). **Manual rotation of the fetal head** is classified under “other procedures to assist delivery,” a parent category that also contains induction of labor by AROM, cervical‑dilation‑based induction, incision of the uterine cervix, manual conversion of uterine position, and related ancillary techniques. Sweeping of membranes is directly linked to AROM as a membrane‑sweeping technique. **Medical induction of labor** (stabilizing induction) and **surgical induction of labor** are organized under “Medical and surgical induction of labor,” with AROM serving as the reference point; surgical induction is an inverse subclass of “medical procedure on cervix,” synonymous

retrieved_docs.content: Manual conversion of position is a medical procedure that is performed on the uterus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 156
retrieved_docs.content: belong to OBSTETRICS: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS and are integrated into routine obstetric care that also encompasses vaginal delivery (with or without episiotomy or forceps), episiotomy repair (categorized under Other procedures associated with delivery), and delivery by vacuum extraction. Symfyzeotomie is identified as a specific form of بضع الارتفاق. Together, these interlinked hierarchies depict a unified system in which Surgical Manipulation encompasses version techniques, assisted‑delivery maneuvers (forceps, double forceps, manual rotation, extraction, vacuum assistance), cervical and membrane interventions (incision of the uterine cervix, artificial rupture of membranes, membrane sweeping), induction strategies (medical, surgical, stabilizing, cervical dilation), termination procedures, uterine abnormalities, and related surgical repairs, all contributing to the full spectrum of obstetric management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2381
retrieved_docs.content: Cesarean hysterectomy, sweeping of membrane, intrapartum fetal stimulation, and manual fetal rotation are all classified as obstetric procedures (إجراء توليدي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2261
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery by double application of forceps and manual rotation of fetal head are obstetric interventions that are performed directly on the fetal head. Both procedures target the fetal head as the site of action, with delivery by double application of forceps used to assist extraction of the baby and manual rotation of fetal head employed to correct malposition. Manual rotation of fetal head is also described as a method of التداول, indicating that it is a technique used to rotate the fetal head into a more favorable orientation during labor. These interconnected procedures are important tools for managing difficult deliveries and optimizing outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 172
retrieved_docs.content: reconstructions—are organized under “other procedures associated with delivery.” Overall, the framework interrelates antenatal/postnatal care, multilingual terminology, instrumental deliveries (forceps, vacuum, breech extraction), fetal‑head manipulations (manual rotation, conversion, versions), induction methods (AROM, membrane sweeping, therapeutic agents), and tissue‑repair procedures, providing a structured model for comprehensive maternal‑fetal management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 137
retrieved_docs.content: Childbirth (الولادة) is presented as the central obstetric event that integrates routine antenatal, intrapartum and post‑natal care (بضع الارتفاق) with a full spectrum of obstetric procedures, assisted‑delivery techniques, surgical manipulations, and acute complications. Routine care comprises ante‑partum care, vaginal delivery of a vertex presentation (with or without episiotomy or forceps) and post‑partum care, and is linked to the presence of medical personnel during the procedure. Delivery methods are organized under the hierarchy “forceps; vacuum; and breech delivery” and include cervical dilatation, forceps delivery (الولادة بالملقط), low‑forceps delivery with episiotomy, delivery by double application of forceps, vacuum extraction (الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين), partial breech extraction, breech delivery in the sitting position without inversion (وضعية المَجيء المقعدي من دون ذكر الانقلاب), external and internal obstetrical versions, manual rotation of the fetal head, manual

retrieved_docs.content: والثدي) and linked by inverse‑ISA relationships to adnexal removal (بضع الارتفاق) and symfyzeotomie. Obstetric incisions form a sub‑category of surgical obstetric actions within the Czech “porodnické chirurgické výkony” group and comprise cardiac puncture (البزل القلبي), amniotic fluid aspiration, vaginal dilation (بضع الفرج) and obstetrical trachelotomy (عملية في عنق الرحم), which is also described as a vaginal cesarean section and a preventive cervical incision. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: **Surgical Manipulation** covers a broad set of labor‑assisting interventions such as artificial rupture of membranes (AROM) to assist delivery, induction of labor by AROM, double‑forceps delivery, delivery of a vertex presentation, internal and combined version without extraction, internal obstetrical version, external obstetrical version, manual conversion of uterine position, manual fetal rotation, intrapartum fetal stimulation, and the preventive cervical incision known as obstetrical trachelotomy (vaginal cesarean section). **Obstetrics Incision** includes amniotomy at delivery, obstetrical trachelotomy, and cardiac puncture (البزل القلبي), reflecting procedures that involve incisional actions on the uterus or cervix. **Other Delivery Procedures** encompass internal and combined version without extraction, external obstetrical version, double‑forceps delivery, manual rotation of the fetal head, failed forceps delivery (فشل التوليد بالملقط), cervical dilation, and vacuum
